coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted polymer functionalized flower-like BiOBr microspheres for photoelectrochemical sensing of chloramphenicol
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zheng
Zhou, Haifei
Jiang, Caiyun
Wang, Yuping
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor showing a high selectivity to for chloramphenicol (CAP) was successfully constructed, based on the molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) functionalized photoelectrochemically active materials. The 3D flower-like BiOBr with large specific surface area was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process and was employed as a matrix to graft the MIPs recognition element (denoted as MIPs-PEC). SEM, TEM, FTIR, XPS, XRD and UV–vis spectroscopy were used to investigate the microstructure characteristics of the as-obtained MIPs-PEC sensor. During the PEC sensing process, MIPs were prepared via a simple thermal polymerization process provided numerous recognition sites, which improved the sensor’s selectivity to CAP. The results showed that photocurrent response signal generated by photo-induced MIPs/BrOBr/ITO electrodes was proportional to the logarithm of CAP concentration over the range from 1.00 ⅹ 10−2 to 1.00 ⅹ 103 ng mL−1 with a low detection limit is 3.02 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3). MIPs-PEC sensor exhibited high selectivity and stability, low cost, and applicability to the determination of CAP in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chloramphenicol
Photoelectrochemical sensing
Flower-like BiOBr
Molecularly imprinted polymers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and fabrication of PEC sensing platform
Characterization of the modified photoactive electrodes
Analytical performance of MIPs/BiOBr/ITO to CAP
Selectivity and stability of the developed MIPs/BiOBr/ITO
Detection of water samples by MIPs-PEC sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of samples (a) BiOBr/ITO, (b) MIPs/BiOBr/ITO and (c) MIPs/BiOBr/ITO; TEM images of samples (d) BiOBr/ITO, (e) MIPs/BiOBr/ITO and (f) MIPs/BiOBr/ITO.

(A) EIS curves of samples (a) BiOBr/ITO, (b) MIP/BrOBr/ITO, (c) MIPs/BrOBr/ITO, (d) NIP/BrOBr/ITO; (B) FT-IR spectra of samples (a) pure CAP (b) MIP, (c) MIPs, and (d) NIP; (C) XRD patterns of BiOBr/ITO and MIPs/BiOBr/ITO samples.
Photocurrent responses of different samples (a) BiOBr/ITO; (b) MIPs/BiOBr/ITO after template eluted; (c) NIP/BiOBr/ITO; (d) MIP/BiOBr/ITO before template eluted.

Effects of Effects of different factors on photocurrent responses (A) BiOBr concentration; (B) applied potential on the photocurrent responses of BiOBr/ITO electrode; (C) AA concentration; (D) modification amount of MIPs on BiOBr/ITO.

(A) Photocurrent responses of the MIP-PEC sensor in different concentrations of OTA from curve a to curve g: the MIPs-PEC sensor was immersed in CAP solutions with concentrations of 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 and1000 ng mL−1; (B) Corresponding calibration curve of the MIPs-PEC for the CAP determination.
(A) Photocurrent responses of 1 ng mL−1 CAP alone and with containing different antibiotics, respectively; (B) Stability of the MIPs-PEC sensor under 20 s on/off irradiation cycles.
Schematic illustration of the formation process of (A) the BiOBr; (B) the MIP and MIPs; (C) the principle of MIPs/BiOBr/ITO PEC sensor for CAP detection.
Detection of CAP in water samples at different concentrations.

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted polymer functionalized flower-like BiOBr microspheres for photoelectrochemical sensing of chloramphenicol

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science
Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Cycling and Pollution Control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, an ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor showing a high selectivity to for chloramphenicol (CAP) was successfully constructed, based on the molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) functionalized photoelectrochemically active materials. The 3D flower-like BiOBr with large specific surface area was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process and was employed as a matrix to graft the MIPs recognition element (denoted as MIPs-PEC). SEM, TEM, FTIR, XPS, XRD and UV–vis spectroscopy were used to investigate the microstructure characteristics of the as-obtained MIPs-PEC sensor. During the PEC sensing process, MIPs were prepared via a simple thermal polymerization process provided numerous recognition sites, which improved the sensor’s selectivity to CAP. The results showed that photocurrent response signal generated by photo-induced MIPs/BrOBr/ITO electrodes was proportional to the logarithm of CAP concentration over the range from 1.00 ⅹ 10−2 to 1.00 ⅹ 103 ng mL−1 with a low detection limit is 3.02 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3). MIPs-PEC sensor exhibited high selectivity and stability, low cost, and applicability to the determination of CAP in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Chloramphenicol (CAP), as a bacteriostatic broad-spectrum antibiotic, can block proteasome synthesis because it can act on the 50S subunit of bacterial ribosomes. Considered as an accessibility and high efficiency against various infectious, it was universally used to treat all kinds of sensitive bacterial infections in human and animal [1]. However, accumulation of excess CAP will cause the development of various diseases, for instance, bone marrow suppression, kidney damage and leukemia [2,3]. The residues of CAP was frequently reported in water samples and animal-derived products due to illegal use [4,5]. In consideration of the health risks of chloramphenicol, many countries have banned its use in animal foods and aquatic products, and its maximum residue limit (MRI) was not allowed to exceed 0.3 μg/kg [6]. At present, the main analysis methods of CAP include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [8], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MC) [9], and electrochemical methods [10,11] and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [12]. Although these detection technologies have advantages in this field, such as HPLC, GC-MC and LC-MC do not need to prepare complex nanomaterials, electrochemical and SERS have high sensitivity, the disadvantages of expensive instruments, complex operations and time-consuming sample preparation are not to ignore. Therefore, it was especially necessary to develop a simple, cheap, easy to operate, high sensitivity and selectivity analytical method with quickly and accurately for CAP determination.

Recently, photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing, as a newly emerged and very promising detection technology, has attracted widespread attention from researchers [13]. Commonly, PEC experiments carry out by the cooperation of electrochemical workstation and light source [14]. During the PEC sensing process, the light source is used to excite and induce the photoactive material on the electrode surface that generates electrons and holes, and then the electrochemical workstation is responsible for recording the detection signal generated by the charge transfer of the photoactive material [15]. Thanks to the complete separation of the light source and the detection signal, PEC sensors have the advantages of low cost, simple operation, fast analysis, easy miniaturization, high sensitivity and low background signals compared to optical and electrochemical technologies [16,17]. Thus, PEC sensors were applied in immunosensors, DNA sensors, RNA sensors, aptasensors, enzymatic analysis, cytosensors, and detection of small molecules and metal ions [18]. It is worth mentioning that the photoelectric conversion capacity of photoelectric active materials affects the signal intensity of photocurrent. Among the numerous semiconductors, Bi-containing semiconductors are widely used in the construction of PEC sensing platform due to their easy synthesis, low cost, good environmental compatibility, high stability and excellent visible activity [19]. As one of Bi-containing semiconductors for PEC beacon, bismuthyl bromide (BiOBr) has attracted a remarkable attention in various applications for unique and excellent chemical stability, good photoelectric activity and charge transfer ability [20]. The band gap energy of BiOBr is about 2.7 V with a four matlockite (PbFCI) layered crystal structure. The layered crystal structure was characterized by Aurivillius-type (Bi(2)O(2))(2+) ([Bi2O2]) slabs and double halogen slabs staggered along the C axis [21]. The special layered structure and moderate ban gap give BiOBr with enhanced separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [22], which is benefit to the strengthening of photoelectric properties and the absorbing of solar energy. Despite these advantages, the hydroxyl radicals or holes generated by BiOBr during photocatalytic oxidation are not suitable for selective analysis of CAP, which is still a challenge to monitor CAP with high sensitivity and selectivity based on BiOBr. Introducing a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) called an “artificial antibody” into BiOBr, and endowing it specific molecular recognition capabilities was considered a good solution. Because of high selectivity and sensitivity, chemical stability, robustness, easy preparation and readily functionalization, the MIP has been employed in manifold analysis fields to construct excellent sensor [23–26]. Therefore, the introduction of molecular imprinting technology into photoelectrochemical analysis technology has significantly enhanced the selectivity of the prepared sensors for specific recognition of target molecules.

In this research, an ingenious MIPs-PEC sensor was successfully constructed for the hypersensitivity detection of CAP. Schematic 1 illustrates the construction of the sensing platform and the PEC sensing mechanism (see Scheme 1 ) First, the flower-shaped microspheres BiOBr formed by stacking the nanosheets was prepared via hydrothermal synthesis method. Then, MIPs were prepared by thermal polymerization and desorption with CAP as template molecule, MAA as functional monomer, EGDMA as crosslinking agent and AIBN as initiator. Subsequently, BiOBr and MIPs were successively modified on the ITO electrode through a layer-by-layer assembly strategy. Finally, MIPs/BiOBr generated photogenerated electron-hole pairs in light illumination, the electron donor cleared the holes in the valence band of BiOBr and the electrons in the conduction band were transferred to the electron donor in the solution. Meanwhile, the electrons on the electrodes were transferred to the holes in the valence band, which formed a periodic photocurrent in a manner similar to a cyclic circuit. With the addition of the template molecule CAP, the imprinted membrane caves on the MIPs/BiOBr/ITO electrodes were recombined with them based on selective adsorption and hydrogen bond recognition. The presence of CAP on MIPs/BiOBr/ITO electrodes enhances the steric hindrance effect of the electrode and hinders the electron transfer between the electrode and the solution, thus resulting in a decrease in photocurrent. As far as we know, this is the first time that MIPs are used to functionalize BiOBr/ITO PEC sensor CAP. The results show that the constructed sensing platform has a wide detection range, low detection limit, good selectivity, stability, and good application prospects.

Chloramphenicol (CAP), ofloxacin (OFL), sulphadimidine (SUL), methacrylic acid (MAA) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Thiamphenicol (TAP), florfenicol (FLR), tetracycline (TCY), azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN), Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EDGMA), acrylic acid (AA) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. supplied bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (BNP), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (HTAB), ethylene glycol (EG), methanol, acetic acid and tetrahydrofuran (THF). All the chemical reagents were used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was prepared from a water purification system (Nanjing Ipuyida Technology Development Co., Ltd.) was used as the solvent.

The crystal structure of the prepared sample was carried out by power X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/max 2500 VL/PC) analysis using CuKα (λ = 1.5406 Å) radiation. The morphologies and microstructures of the obtained products were analyzed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-5610LV) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM ZEOL JEM-2100). Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra of the polymer were obtained on the Perkin-Elemer PE-983 spectrometer. The absorption range the catalysts were measured by a Cary 5000 Spectrophotometer (American Aglent) with BaSO4 as a reflectance. The absorbance of samples at the wavelength 200 nm–800 nm was recorded by Cary 5000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian Co., USA). Performing the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum measured on Thermo Fisher Ecsalab 250xi was used to study the structure of the sample. The amperometric (I - t) curves was obtained in an CHI660E electrochemical workstation（Shanghai, CH Instruments, China）with a traditional three electrode under the irradiation of a 350W xenon lamp. Among them, Indium tin oxide (ITO, 1 ⅹ 2.5 cm) as a working electrode was purchased from Jiangsu Jintan kangdake Photoelectric Technology Co., Ltd, and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode and a platinum column as a counter electrode were purchased from Tianjin Aida Hengcheng Instrument Co., Ltd. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted over the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz at 0.24 V DC potential superimposed on xx V AC voltage perturbation in 5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- containing 0.1 mol L−1 KCl solution.

BiOBr was prepared by a simple hydrothermal synthesis method. In the typical process, 2 mmol of HTAB was dissolved to 60 mL of 40 °C EG solution under the continuous stirring. Next, 2 mmol BNP added into the above mixed solution and continue stirring for 1 h. Subsequently, the suspension was transferred into a 100 mL of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, was sealed and heated at 160 °C for 12 h. After the hydrothermal reaction was completed and cooled to room temperature, the sediment was collected by centrifuged and washed alternately with deionized water and absolute ethanol. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed alternately with distilled water and absolute ethanol, then dried under oven at 65 °C for 12 h.

Molecularly imprinted polymers were prepared by thermal polymerization. Briefly, the functional monomer 150 μL MAA and the template molecule CAP 0.1 g were prepolymerized in a centrifuge tube. 670 μL THF was added into the mixture for ultrasonic dispersion until the reaction solution was clear and transparent. Following that, 500 μL of EDGMA as crosslinker and 50 mg of AIBN as initiator were dissolved in the previous mixture by sonication for 15 min. The polymerization reaction was carried out via placing in a constant temperature water bath at 60 °C for 2 h. The ultimate product was ground and named MIP. For comparison, non-imprinted polymer labeled NIP was obtained according to the identical method without CAP. Desorption of MIP was performed by continuous centrifugation with methanol/acetic acid (V/V = 9:1) until CAP in the eluent solution was not detected at 270 nm by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The as-obtained sample was washed and was dried, and denoted as MIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of PEC sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The ITO electrode (sheet resistance, 20–25 Ω/square) was cleaned by successive sonication in acetone, ethanol and pure water for 15 min each before use. Then, 10 mg of BiOBr powder was dispersed ultrasonically in 5 mL of water, and 20 μL of the resulting suspension (5.0 mg mL−1) was evenly applied on the surface of ITO glass with a fixed area of 0.28 cm2, and then it was naturally dried to room temperature. The obtained modified ITO glass was marked as BiOBr/ITO electrode. Afterwards, 15 μL of MIPs (4.0 mg mL−1) was immobilized on the surface of BiOBr/ITO electrode and was dried at room temperature. The obtained MIPs/BiOBr/ITO electrode was washed completely with water to detach surface unstable polymer. As a control, MIP/BiOBr/ITO and NIP/BiOBr/ITO electrodes were also fabricated according to the same assembly method.

The constructed sensor was incubated with different concentrations of CAP solution at 30 °C for 30 min. The PEC sensing experiment was performed in Tris-HCl buffered saline (0.1 M, pH 7.4) solution containing AA (0.05 M) at a bias voltage of −0.1 V. The current−time plots were recorded by switching the lights in turn for 20 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the modified photoactive electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: SEM and TEM observed the morphology and microstructure of BiOBr/ITO, MIPs/BiOBr/ITO and MIPs/BiOBr/ITO. As described in Fig. 1 A, the BiOBr sample displayed flower-sphere three-dimensional structure with different sizes. Fig. 1D was the TEM image of BiOBr. The flower-like microsphere structure formed via the nanosheet self-assembly was clearly observed. Closer observation manifests that BiOBr nanosheets were approximately 20 nm thick. Fig. 1B and E reveals that massive MIPs were made up of small particles with a size between 50 and 100 nm. The scattered particles may be attributed to the cross-linking of the template molecule and the functional monomer under the action of the cross-linking agent. In Fig. 1C, the modification of MIPs did not affect the structure of flower-like BiOBr microspheres. Fig. 1F exhibited that BiOBr covered by imprinted membrane confirms the formation of MIP films on the surface of BiOBr/ITO. The flower-shaped BiOBr formed by stacking nanosheets has a large specific surface area, which can load more MIPs, and accelerated the recognition of target molecules.

EIS was used to examine the interfacial electron transfer capabilities of different modified electrodes and to monitor the manufacturing process of PEC sensors [27]. The results of EIS experiments using [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- as a redox probe were shown in Fig. 2 A. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) was represented by the high-frequency semicircle region of the Nyquist diagram. It was unambiguous to see that the individual BiOBr/ITO electrode had a smaller semicircle (curve a). When the MIP of the imprinted CAP was coated on surface of the BiOBr/ITO electrode, the semicircle was significantly increased (curve b), which may be due to the imprinting membrane with poor conductivity obstruct electron transfer. This further confirms that the MIP was successfully loaded onto the flower-shaped BiOBr. After removing the template of CAP, the semicircles decreased (curve c). The results suggest that the modification of the imprinted membrane hinders the contact of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe with the electrode surface, and the imprinted membrane holes formed by the removal of CAP opened the door for the diffusion of ferricyanide [28,29]. In addition, the semicircle after removing the CAP almost coincides with the semicircle of the NIP/BiOBr/ITO electrode (curve d), and the side illustrates the complete removal of the template molecules. The above EIS examination results demonstrated that the successfully prepared MIP-PEC sensor could specifically identify CAP.

In order to evidence the presence of polymer functional groups and the successful mosaic of the template molecule, Fourier infrared (FT-IR) spectra of different samples were recorded and the results showed in Fig. 2B. The similar characteristic peaks in curve b, c and d (MIPs, MIP and NIP, respectively) of Fig. 2B can be observed. For example, 3450 cm−1 (corresponding to N–H stretching vibration peak), 2993 cm−1 (corresponding to C–H symmetric and asymmetric stretching), 1729 cm−1 (corresponding to CO amide stretching vibration), 1638 cm−1 (corresponding to N–H bending vibration), 1460 cm−1 (corresponding to C–H in-plane bending vibration) and 1160 cm−1 (C–N stretching vibration), etc. The results were consistent with the characteristic absorption peaks of MAA and EDGMA in the related literature [30,31]. The FTIR spectra of MIP and CAP (curve a) had distinct characteristic absorption peaks at 812 cm−1 compared to NIP, which was attributed to the out-of-plane vibration of C–H in the CAP molecule [32]. Interestingly, the band of MIP at 3442 cm−1 (N–H stretching vibrations) was watched to have significant enhancements and slight offsets. This indicated that the N–H groups of the MIP could form hydrogen bonds with CAP. When the template molecule was removed, the characteristic absorption peak containing CAP completely disappeared, and the infrared spectrum became the same as NIP. FTIR measurements demonstrated that the templates and monomer had been successfully polyerized.

XRD patterns of BiOBr and MIPs/BiOBr on ITO substrate was shown in Fig. 2C. The main diffraction peaks of material at 25.2°, 31.7°, 32.2°, 57.1° and other peaks were consistent with that of the tetragonal BiOBr standard PDF cards. (JCPDS card No. 09–0393). It was noteworthy that BiOBr/ITO and MIPs/BiOBr/ITO had identical diffraction peaks. It showed that the introduction of MIPs did not affect the crystal structure of BiOBr. The ability of photoactive materials to absorb light greatly affects the photoelectrochemical properties, and the optical properties of BiOBr/ITO and MIPs/BiOBr/ITO had been studied. As depicted in Fig. S1, the two curves had almost the same absorption spectrum, confirming that they can absorb photons in the total ultraviolet and visible light regions.

The elemental composition and surface chemical state of the as obtained MIPs/BiOBr were analysis through XPS investigation. Fig. S2A displayed the survey spectrum of the prepared materials, MIPs/BiOBr contains four elements, Br, Bi, O, and C, and there were no impurity energy peaks. In Fig. S2B, the peaks situated at 68 eV and 69.1 eV were defined to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2, indicating that the Br element in the obtained material exists in the form of Br−. The high-resolution spectra of Bi was shown in Fig. S2C, the strong absorption peaks at 159.0 eV and 164.3 eV correspond to the Bi 4f 5/2 and Bi 4f 7/2, matching well with the standard satellite peak of Bi3+ [33,34]. As shown in Fig. S2D, the O1s high-resolution spectrum of MIPs/BiOBr can be fitted to three peaks at 529.8 eV, 531.6 eV, and 533.2 eV, which were attributed to the lattice oxygen of BiOBr (Bi–O), the lattice oxygen of methacrylic acid in MIPs, and other components such as water [35,36]. This XPS experiment confirmed once again that the composite material was composed of BiOBr and polymer.

The photoinduced behavior of different modified electrodes was compared through photocurrent response. As displayed in Fig. 3 , the BiOBr/ITO electrode exhibits the largest photocurrent response signal compared to other electrodes (curve a). The subsequent modification of non-imprinted NIP onto the surface of BiOBr/ITO electrode induced reduces in photocurrent (curve c). When the MIP carrying the template molecule was modified on the BiOBr/ITO surface, a weak photocurrent signal was observed (curve d). These results were due to the poor conductivity of the imprinted polymer. Once the template molecules were eluted in the MIP, the photocurrent response intensity increased largely (curve b), but still did not exceed the BiOBr/ITO electrode. This was because the exposure of caves on the imprinted film accelerated the electrons transfer and the diffusion of electron donors. Based on the changes in the photocurrent response in the presence or absence of CAP, MIPs-PEC sensors were successfully constructed.

In order to obtain the best analysis performance of PEC sensor for CAP detection, some experimental parameters were optimized. As shown in Fig. 4 A, it can be clearly seen that as the BiOBr concentration increased from 1 to 5 mg mL−1, the photocurrent response increased significantly. However, with the increased of BiOBr concentration from 5 to 10 mg mL−1, we found this response was attenuated. This may be because the excess BiOBr on the electrode surface inhibiting inhibited pho to-generated electron–hole separation and transport. Therefore, the optimal BiOBr concentration was specified as 5 mg mL−1 for the PEC experiment. This PEC response also depended on the bias voltage because it affected the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Fig. 4B showed the photocurrent response under different application potentials. It can be seen that when the applied potential increased toward the negative bias direction from 0.1 to −0.3 V, this photocurrent showed an upward trend. The increasing degree of the photocurrent with the applied potential in the range of 0.1 to −0.1 V was higher than the range of −0.1 to −0.3 V. Moreover, the high application potential was liable to destroy the structure of the photoactive material. Thus, −0.1 V were selected as the optimal application potentials in this work.

During PEC experiments, AA as an electron donor can capture holes to improve the photocurrent intensity and maintain the stability of the photocurrent. To obtain the best photocurrent response signal, PEC experiments were performed at different concentrations of AA. From Fig. 4C, the photocurrent signal intensity was gradually promoted with the AA concentration increased. When the AA concentration reached 0.05 M, the photocurrent signal hardly changed as the AA concentration increased. Thus, 0.05 M was selected as the optimal concentration. As the recognition element, the amount of MIPs dispersed on the surface of the BiOBr/ITO electrode was also an important factor that influences the PEC response. With more MIPs, there were more recognition sites, which leaded to an increase in photocurrent changes.

Fig. 4Dshowed that as the amount of MIPs increased, the photocurrent response was decreased due to the increasing steric hindrance effect, and then increases due to the exfoliation of excessively thick MIPs films during the experiment. Thus, the amount of 15 μL MIPs dispersion was the optimal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of MIPs/BiOBr/ITO to CAP
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the above optimal conditions, the prepared MIPs-PEC sensors were applied to the determination of various CAP concentrations. As presented in Fig. 5 A, a higher photocurrent response signal was observed without the binding target. With the increased of CAP concentration in the immersion solution from 0.01 to 1000 ng mL−1, the photocurrent response gradually decreased, which indicated that the enhancement of the steric hindrance effect hindered the electrons transfer. Fig. 5B showed that the photocurrent response of the MIPs-PEC sensor had a negative correlation with the logarithm value of the CAP concentration. The corresponding linear regression equation was I (μA) = −0.0533log (c, ng mL−1) + 0.711 (R2 = 0.997). The detection limit of this sensor was also calculated according to the following formula [37]:
L O D = 3
S B
/ m
Where SB was the standard deviation of the blank solution, and m was the slope of calibration curve. The detection limit was estimated to be 3.02 pg mL−1. In addition, the prepared MIPs-PEC sensor had a wider detection range and lower detection limit compared with the CAP detection method reported in related literature (Table S1) [38–43]. By contrast, the application of molecular imprinting technology to photoelectrochemical sensing was comparable or better in terms of monitoring pollutants in the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the developed MIPs/BiOBr/ITO
ce:section.ce:para: The as-obtained MIPs-PEC sensor’s selective detection capability was evaluated by using molecules of similar structure as interference antibiotics. The interference experiment was performed in a 1 ng mL−1 CAP solution by adding 1 ng mL−1 other antibiotics such as TAP, FLR, SUL, OFL, TCY. As can be seen in Fig. 6 A, the photocurrent response of different antibiotics added to solution in the presence of CAP was almost unchanged, indicating that the imprinted membrane on the prepared sensor can successfully distinguish CAP from other types of antibiotics via cave size, electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bond recognition. The result demonstrated that the prepared MIPs-PEC sensors exhibited the superior selectivity toward CAP. Simultaneously, stability was also an important factor for sensor applications. As shown in Fig. 6B, the photocurrent response of MIPs/BiOBr/ITO electrode under 20 times on/off light irradiation cycles. It can be observed that the photocurrent response signal does not change significantly during repeated switching of the lamp within 1000 s. Obviously, the constructed MIPs-PEC sensing platform had an excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water samples by MIPs-PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practical application ability of the prepared PEC sensor, tap water taken from laboratory water pipes was tested. Before measurements, the collected tap water should be filtered through a Millipore filter (0.45 μm).Then the samples were spiked with different concentration levels of CAP, and the method proposed above was used to detect these samples. The results were listed in Table 1 , the average recoveries range were from 94% to 106.1% with relative standard deviations between 3.8% and 5.7%. Consequently, the sensor had an acceptable feasibility for the detection of CAP in actual water samples, and had a good application prospect as an alternative to the actual water sample monitoring in the environment.

In summary, a photoelectrochemical sensor was developed based on 3D flower-like BiOBr with high photocurrent response activity and MIPs with a large number of recognition elements. It was demonstrated that the MIPs/BiOBr electrode as a photo-induced sensor successfully implements CAP sensitivity detection. Meanwhile, the fabricated MIPs-PEC sensor platform presented a wider linear range and lower detection limit than that of the past reported. Besides, the sensor manifested good sensitivity, selectivity, stability and feasibility. Moreover, photoelectrochemical technology and molecular imprinting technology were combined to integrated MIPs-PEC sensor, which was simple and cost-effective fabrication while the detection process was very easy, and had a rapid response time. Importantly, the concept provides a viable strategy and attracted more attention in the design, development, and practical application of photoelectrochemical sensing platforms for the detection of other antibiotics.
Zheng Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Haifei Zhou: Validation, Data curation. Caiyun Jiang: Supervision. Yuping Wang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 51578295), the National Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161479), and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Chemical Pollution Control and Resources Reuse (Nanjing University of Science and Technology), Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province. Additionally, we also appreciate Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials and a project funded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2020.136161.
